tagged_story_for_tts:
Stories shaped like weapons.<break time=800ms/> Truth stretched like taffy.<break time=800ms/>

The phrase media manipulation names the act of twisting information—like bending a spoon until it reflects light in ways that distort what’s real.<break time=800ms/> It doesn’t create lies from scratch;<break time=200ms/> it repurposes facts, omits context, or amplifies emotions to steer how people think.<break time=800ms/>

Here’s how it works:<break time=100ms/> A single image of a protest, cropped to hide the peaceful crowd, becomes "proof" of chaos.<break time=800ms/> A politician’s old quote, replayed out of sequence, seems to endorse the opposite of what they said.<break time=800ms/> Algorithms don’t invent bias—they detect which slanted headline makes you linger 0.3 seconds longer, then feed you more.<break time=800ms/> In 2016, Macedonian teens earned thousands by fabricating pro-Trump fake news, not because they cared about politics, but because outrage paid $5 per click.<break time=800ms/>

The tools are simple:<break time=100ms/> framing (calling taxes "investments" or "theft"), selective editing (showing only the moment a scientist hesitates), and false balance (giving equal weight to 97 climate scientists and 3 oil lobbyists).<break time=800ms/> The goal isn’t to convince you of a lie—it’s to make you question the truth until you disengage entirely.<break time=800ms/>

Three related subjects are cognitive dissonance tricks, algorithm-fed echo chambers, and how wars use radio waves.<break time=800ms/>
clean_story:
Stories shaped like weapons. Truth stretched like taffy.

The phrase media manipulation names the act of twisting information—like bending a spoon until it reflects light in ways that distort what’s real. It doesn’t create lies from scratch; it repurposes facts, omits context, or amplifies emotions to steer how people think.

Here’s how it works: A single image of a protest, cropped to hide the peaceful crowd, becomes "proof" of chaos. A politician’s old quote, replayed out of sequence, seems to endorse the opposite of what they said. Algorithms don’t invent bias—they detect which slanted headline makes you linger 0.3 seconds longer, then feed you more. In 2016, Macedonian teens earned thousands by fabricating pro-Trump fake news, not because they cared about politics, but because outrage paid $5 per click.

The tools are simple: framing (calling taxes "investments" or "theft"), selective editing (showing only the moment a scientist hesitates), and false balance (giving equal weight to 97 climate scientists and 3 oil lobbyists). The goal isn’t to convince you of a lie—it’s to make you question the truth until you disengage entirely.

Three related subjects are cognitive dissonance tricks, algorithm-fed echo chambers, and how wars use radio waves.
